examples.title: Basic Date Display and Formatting
examples.description: Demonstrates various date formatting options and storing date in variables
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Display current date in different formats
echo "Default format: $(date)"
echo "ISO 8601: $(date +%Y-%m-%d)"
echo "US format: $(date +%m/%d/%Y)"
echo "European format: $(date +%d-%m-%Y)"
echo "With time: $(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")"

# Store date in variables
CURRENT_DATE=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
CURRENT_TIME=$(date +%H:%M:%S)
EPOCH=$(date +%s)

echo "Stored date: $CURRENT_DATE"
echo "Stored time: $CURRENT_TIME"
echo "Epoch timestamp: $EPOCH"
examples.explanation: This script demonstrates basic date command usage: default output, custom formatting with format specifiers, storing date in variables using command substitution $(), and displaying epoch timestamp.

examples.title: Date Arithmetic and Conversion
examples.description: Shows how to perform date calculations and convert between formats
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Display relative dates
echo "Yesterday: $(date -d "yesterday")"
echo "Tomorrow: $(date -d "tomorrow")"
echo "Next week: $(date -d "next week")"
echo "Last month: $(date -d "last month")"
echo "2 years ago: $(date -d "2 years ago")"

# Convert epoch timestamp to readable date
EPOCH=1704067200
echo "Epoch $EPOCH is: $(date -d @$EPOCH)"
echo "Same in UTC: $(date -ud @$EPOCH)"

# Display specific format from epoch
FORMATTED=$(date -d @$EPOCH +"%A, %B %d, %Y at %H:%M:%S")
echo "Formatted: $FORMATTED"

# Day of week for a specific date
date -d "2024-01-15" +"%A"
examples.explanation: This script demonstrates date arithmetic using the -d option with relative strings, converting Unix epoch timestamps to human-readable format using @ symbol, and displaying specific date information like day of week.

examples.title: Log File Timestamp Generator
examples.description: Creates timestamped log entries commonly used in shell scripts
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Function to generate timestamped log messages
log_message() {
 local level=$1
 local message=$2
 local timestamp=$(date +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
 echo "[$timestamp] [$level] $message"
}

# Generate log entries
log_message "INFO" "Application started"
log_message "DEBUG" "Loading configuration file"
log_message "ERROR" "Failed to connect to database"
log_message "INFO" "Application terminated normally"

# Create a timestamped backup filename
BACKUP_FILE="backup_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).tar.gz"
echo "Backup would be saved as: $BACKUP_FILE"

# Display file modification time
FILE="example.txt"
if [ -f "$FILE" ]; then
 echo "File $FILE was last modified:"
 date -r "$FILE" +"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"
else
 echo "File $FILE does not exist"
fi
examples.explanation: This practical script shows how to use the date command for logging in shell scripts. It demonstrates creating timestamped log messages with level indicators, generating unique backup filenames with timestamps, and using -r option to display file modification times.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for log entries, O(1) for others
